activities_projects.title: Particle Nature of Matter
activities_projects.objective: To prove that matter is made up of particles
steps: Take a 100 mL beaker and fill it halfway with water [cite: 2341]
Mark the initial level of water on the beaker [cite: 2341]
Add a spoonful of salt and stir well with a glass rod [cite: 2342]
Observe if the water level rises or stays the same [cite: 2343]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that salt particles occupy the spaces between water particles [cite: 2352]
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct observation of water level and explanation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Smallness of Particles
activities_projects.objective: To demonstrate how small the particles of matter are
steps: Dissolve 2-3 crystals of potassium permanganate in 100 mL water [cite: 2356]
Take 10 mL of this deep colored solution and add it to 90 mL clear water [cite: 3260]
Repeat this dilution process 5 to 8 times [cite: 2362]
Observe if the water is still colored [cite: 2363]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that a few crystals contain millions of tiny particles [cite: 2386]
activities_projects.evaluation: Recording color intensity changes across dilutions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Diffusion in Gases
activities_projects.objective: To observe the movement of gas particles
steps: Place an unlit incense stick in a corner and try to smell it [cite: 2372, 2373]
Light the incense stick using a matchbox [cite: 2374]
Check if you can get the smell while sitting at a distance [cite: 2374]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Observes that particles move faster when kinetic energy increases [cite: 2413, 2414]
activities_projects.evaluation: Explanation of how smell spreads in the room (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Intermixing of Liquids
activities_projects.objective: To compare the rate of diffusion in different liquids
steps: Put a drop of blue/red ink slowly along the side of the first beaker [cite: 2396]
Put a drop of honey in the same way in the second beaker [cite: 2396]
Leave them undisturbed and record the time taken to spread evenly [cite: 2397, 2400]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that different liquids diffuse at different rates [cite: 2406]
activities_projects.evaluation: Comparing the diffusion time of ink vs honey (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Temperature and Diffusion
activities_projects.objective: To study the effect of temperature on the rate of mixing
steps: Drop a crystal into a glass of hot water [cite: 2403]
Drop a crystal into a glass of cold water at the same time [cite: 2403]
Observe the rate of mixing in both without stirring [cite: 2404]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Discovers that diffusion becomes faster on heating [cite: 2418]
activities_projects.evaluation: Explaining why hot water mixes faster (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Force of Attraction
activities_projects.objective: To understand that particles of matter attract each other
steps: Try breaking the iron nail by hammering [cite: 2426, 2427]
Try breaking the chalk by cutting it [cite: 2427]
Try breaking the rubber band by stretching it [cite: 2427]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that the strength of attraction varies between substances [cite: 2443]
activities_projects.evaluation: Identifying which substance has the strongest force (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Compressibility Test
activities_projects.objective: To compare compressibility of solids, liquids, and gases
steps: Close syringe nozzles with rubber corks [cite: 2513]
Fill one with air, second with water, and third with chalk [cite: 2515]
Push the pistons and observe which is easiest to compress [cite: 2517]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Observes that gases are highly compressible compared to solids/liquids [cite: 2518]
activities_projects.evaluation: Ranking the three states by compressibility (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Sublimation Observation
activities_projects.objective: To see solid changing directly to gas
steps: Put crushed camphor in a china dish and cover with an inverted funnel [cite: 2637, 2638]
Plug the funnel stem with cotton [cite: 2639]
Heat slowly and observe the changes inside the funnel [cite: 2652]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Defines sublimation as change from solid to gas without liquid phase [cite: 2653]
activities_projects.evaluation: Drawing the sublimation setup and labeling (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Evaporation and Surface Area
activities_projects.objective: To see how surface area affects evaporation
steps: Take 5 mL water in a test tube and 5 mL in an open china dish [cite: 2704, 2705]
Place both under a fan [cite: 2705]
Record the time taken for water to evaporate in each [cite: 2708]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that increasing surface area increases the rate of evaporation [cite: 2714]
activities_projects.evaluation: Data table showing evaporation times (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Evaporation and Cooling
activities_projects.objective: To experience the cooling effect of evaporation
steps: Pour a few drops of acetone on your palm [cite: 2728]
Wait for a few seconds [cite: 2729]
Note the sensation on your palm [cite: 2729]
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that particles absorb energy from surroundings to evaporate [cite: 2724, 2725]
activities_projects.evaluation: Scientific explanation of the cold sensation (10 marks)
